---
title: "Session 7: Let the Data Predict"
subtitle: "Business Intelligence & AI"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
---

## From "What Happened?" to "What Will Happen?" {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Welcome back. Over the past six sessions you have learned to generate analyses with AI, query enterprise databases, build reporting pipelines, and evaluate reliability. All of that answers the question "what happened?" Today we add a fundamentally different capability: prediction. Instead of looking backward at your data, we ask it to look forward. By the end of this session, you will have built a predictive model yourself --- using AI to handle the code --- and you will know how to evaluate whether the predictions are trustworthy.

Before we get into prediction, let us debrief the Atherton case you read for homework. The governance failures in that case connect directly to what we are doing today.

[TIMING: 1 minute. This is the opening section divider. Move immediately to the Atherton debrief.]
:::

---

## Atherton Case Debrief {.shrink}

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Atherton's Document AI Failures
:::

- **Chunking failures** --- long contract clauses split mid-sentence, answers cited incomplete text
- **Access control gaps** --- junior analysts saw board-level compensation data
- **Stale documents** --- system answered from a superseded compliance policy
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
The Parallel to Predictive Models
:::

- Stale training data = stale predictions
- Access control still matters --- who sees predicted churn scores? Predicted salaries?
- Garbage in, garbage out applies to ML just as much as to document AI
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
[ATHERTON DEBRIEF (8 minutes, minutes 0-8): Start by asking the room: "What was the most damaging failure in the Atherton case --- chunking, access control, or staleness?" Call on 3-4 students. Push for specificity: "You said staleness --- what specifically went wrong? What was the business impact of answering from a superseded policy?"

Listen for the themes: (1) Atherton did not version their documents, so the system could not distinguish current from superseded policies. (2) Access controls that existed in SharePoint did not carry over to the AI system. (3) Chunking is a technical problem that has business consequences --- a contract clause split mid-sentence produces a wrong answer that looks right.

Then make the bridge: "Every one of these failures has a direct parallel in predictive modeling. If your training data is stale --- say you trained a churn model on 2023 behavior but your pricing changed in 2024 --- your predictions are answering from a superseded version of reality. If anyone in the organization can see predicted attrition scores for individual employees, you have the same access control problem Atherton had. And if your input data is messy --- duplicate records, missing values, inconsistent formats --- that is the ML equivalent of chunking failures. The garbage goes in, and confident-looking predictions come out."]

[TIMING: 8 minutes total. Then bridge to prediction with: "With that context, let us talk about what prediction actually means in a business setting."]
:::

---

## Today's Learning Objectives

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
By the end of this session, you will be able to:
:::

- Distinguish classification from regression and identify which applies to a business problem
- Use AI to build and evaluate a gradient boosting model without writing code yourself
- Interpret a confusion matrix, feature importance, and cross-validation scores
- Apply the maker/checker framework to decide whether predictions are trustworthy
:::

::: {.notes}
Four objectives today. First, you will learn the two types of prediction --- classification and regression --- and be able to categorize any business prediction problem. Second, you will use Claude to build a real predictive model. Third, you will learn to read the outputs that tell you whether the model is any good. And fourth, you will apply the same maker/checker framework from Session 2, but now to model evaluation instead of document verification.

The key insight for today: you do not need to understand the math. You need to understand what the model produces and how to evaluate it. AI handles the code; you handle the judgment.

[TIMING: 1 minute. Move to the two types of prediction.]
:::

---

## Two Types of Prediction {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Let us start with the most fundamental distinction in predictive modeling. Every business prediction falls into one of two categories, and knowing which one you are dealing with determines everything that follows --- the model you build, the metrics you use, and how you interpret the results.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Classification vs. Regression

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Classification: "Which category?"
:::

- Will this customer churn? [Yes / No]{.amber}
- Is this transaction fraudulent? [Fraud / Legitimate]{.amber}
- Which segment does this lead belong to? [Hot / Warm / Cold]{.amber}
- Will this employee leave within 12 months?
- Will this loan default?
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Regression: "How much?"
:::

- How much will this customer spend next quarter? [$12,400]{.accent}
- What will demand be in March? [4,200 units]{.accent}
- What price maximizes margin? [$89.99]{.accent}
- How many support tickets will we get next week?
- What will Q3 revenue be?
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
Every business prediction is one of these two types. The type determines how you build the model and how you evaluate it.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is the fundamental split. Classification predicts categories --- yes or no, fraud or legitimate, hot or warm or cold. Regression predicts continuous numbers --- dollar amounts, quantities, prices.

The distinction matters because the evaluation is completely different. For classification, you care about things like: how often does the model say "churn" when the customer actually stays? That is a false positive, and it has a business cost --- you might offer an unnecessary discount. For regression, you care about how far off the predictions are on average.

Let me give you a quick exercise. I am going to name five business questions, and I want you to tell me: classification or regression?

[AUDIENCE EXERCISE (2 minutes): Read each question, pause for hands or shout-outs:
1. "Will this prospect close?" --- Classification
2. "What will this deal be worth?" --- Regression
3. "Which customers are at risk of downgrading?" --- Classification
4. "How many returns will we process in January?" --- Regression
5. "Should we approve this credit application?" --- Classification

After the exercise: "Good. Notice that the same business domain --- say, sales --- can have both types. 'Will this deal close?' is classification. 'What will it be worth?' is regression. The type depends on the question, not the domain."]

[TIMING: 5 minutes total including the exercise. Move to gradient boosting.]
:::

---

## Common Business Prediction Problems {.shrink}

::: {.comparison-table}

| Problem | Type | Input Data | Business Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer churn | Classification | Usage patterns, support tickets, tenure | Retain at-risk customers before they leave |
| Lead scoring | Classification | Demographics, engagement, firmographics | Focus sales effort on highest-probability leads |
| Fraud detection | Classification | Transaction amount, location, velocity | Block fraudulent transactions in real time |
| Revenue forecasting | Regression | Historical revenue, pipeline, seasonality | Accurate budgeting and resource allocation |
| Demand planning | Regression | Sales history, promotions, weather | Optimize inventory and reduce stockouts |
| Dynamic pricing | Regression | Competitor prices, demand signals, elasticity | Maximize margin without losing volume |

:::

::: {.notes}
This table shows six common prediction problems you will encounter in business. The left three are classification --- they predict categories. The right three are regression --- they predict numbers. Notice the pattern: classification problems tend to drive operational decisions (retain this customer, flag this transaction, prioritize this lead), while regression problems tend to drive planning decisions (how much to budget, how much to stock, what price to set).

For your breakout later, you will identify prediction problems at your own company. Use this table as a starting point, but do not limit yourself to these six.

[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to gradient boosting.]
:::

---

## Gradient Boosting: The One Method {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
We are not going to survey dozens of algorithms. There is one method that dominates tabular prediction --- meaning prediction from spreadsheet-style data with rows and columns --- and that is gradient boosting. This is what practitioners reach for first, what wins most Kaggle competitions on structured data, and what you will use today. Let me explain how it works conceptually.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Why One Method? {.shrink}

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
70%+
:::
::: {.stat-label}
of Kaggle competition winners on tabular data use gradient boosting
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
XGBoost
:::
::: {.stat-label}
most popular implementation --- used by every major tech company
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
#1
:::
::: {.stat-label}
first choice for practitioners on real-world business data
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
Deep learning dominates images and language. For [structured business data]{.accent} --- the kind in your databases and spreadsheets --- gradient boosting wins.
:::

::: {.notes}
Why are we teaching you one method instead of ten? Because gradient boosting dominates. Over seventy percent of Kaggle competition winners on tabular data --- meaning data that looks like a spreadsheet --- use some form of gradient boosting. XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost are the three most popular implementations, and they are used by every major tech company.

You have probably heard about deep learning and neural networks. Those dominate images, text, and language --- which is why ChatGPT and Claude are so impressive. But for structured business data --- rows and columns, the kind of data in your CRM, your ERP, your financial systems --- gradient boosting consistently outperforms neural networks. It handles messy real-world data well: missing values, mixed data types, skewed distributions.

You do not need to master the implementation. Claude will write the code. But you need to understand the concept so you can evaluate vendor claims and ask the right questions when someone says "we built a machine learning model."

This statistic is directionally correct for structured/tabular data competitions through the mid-2020s. The key point is that gradient boosting consistently outperforms other methods on the kind of spreadsheet and database data you work with.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the intuition slide.]
:::

---

## How Gradient Boosting Works {.shrink}

::: {.step-flow}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🌳
:::
::: {.step-title}
Tree 1
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Build a small decision tree. It gets some predictions right, some wrong.
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🎯
:::
::: {.step-title}
Find Errors
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Look at what Tree 1 got wrong. Focus on those mistakes.
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🌳🌳
:::
::: {.step-title}
Tree 2
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Build a new tree that specifically corrects Tree 1's errors.
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🔄
:::
::: {.step-title}
Repeat
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Keep adding trees. Each one fixes the remaining errors. Stop when improvement plateaus.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**The key insight:** No single tree is smart. But hundreds of trees, each correcting the previous one's mistakes, produce remarkably accurate predictions. This is [ensemble learning]{.accent} --- the wisdom of many weak learners.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is the conceptual explanation. Gradient boosting builds many small decision trees sequentially. The first tree makes predictions --- some right, some wrong. The second tree does not start from scratch; it focuses specifically on the cases the first tree got wrong. The third tree corrects the remaining errors from tree two. And so on, for hundreds or thousands of trees.

No single tree is particularly smart. Each one is a "weak learner" --- it might be only slightly better than random guessing. But the combination of hundreds of weak learners, each correcting the previous one's mistakes, produces a model that is remarkably accurate. This is called ensemble learning.

Think of it like editing a document. The first draft has errors. A second pass catches some of those errors but might miss others. A third pass catches what the second missed. After enough passes, the document is polished. That is gradient boosting --- many passes, each one cleaning up what the previous pass missed.

You do not need to remember any of this to use the technique. Claude will handle the implementation. But when a vendor tells you "we built a machine learning model for churn prediction," you now know enough to ask: "What algorithm? Gradient boosting? How many trees? What was the cross-validation score?" Those questions signal that you are not someone who can be sold a black box.

[TIMING: 5 minutes. Move to the ML workflow.]
:::

---

## What Is a Decision Tree? {.shrink}

::: {.highlight-box}
A decision tree asks a series of yes/no questions about your data to reach a prediction:
:::

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Example: Will this customer churn?
:::
Is monthly usage < 5 sessions?
├── YES → Has tenure < 12 months?
│   ├── YES → **CHURN** (85% confidence)
│   └── NO  → Has open support ticket?
│       ├── YES → **CHURN** (70% confidence)
│       └── NO  → **STAY** (60% confidence)
└── NO  → Has usage declined 3 months in a row?
    ├── YES → **CHURN** (65% confidence)
    └── NO  → **STAY** (90% confidence)
:::

::: {.explainer}
Each tree in gradient boosting is [small and simple]{.accent} --- maybe 4-8 questions deep. The power comes from combining hundreds of these simple trees.
:::

::: {.notes}
Before we move on, let me make decision trees concrete. A decision tree is just a series of yes/no questions. Is monthly usage less than five sessions? If yes, go left. Has the customer been with us less than a year? If yes, predict churn with eighty-five percent confidence.

Each individual tree in a gradient boosting model is deliberately kept small --- maybe four to eight questions deep. This is by design. A deep, complex tree would memorize the training data instead of learning generalizable patterns. By keeping each tree simple and combining hundreds of them, gradient boosting gets the best of both worlds: complexity in aggregate, simplicity in each component.

You will see this in action in a few minutes when we build a model with Claude.

[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to the ML workflow section.]
:::

---

## The ML Workflow {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Now let us walk through the end-to-end workflow for building a predictive model. This is the sequence you will follow in the hands-on exercise, and it is the same sequence that data scientists follow in production. The difference is that you will have Claude write the code while you focus on evaluating the output.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Five Steps to a Predictive Model

::: {.step-flow-5}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
📊
:::
::: {.step-title}
Prepare Data
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Clean, explore, handle missing values
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
✂️
:::
::: {.step-title}
Split
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Train set (80%) and test set (20%)
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
⚙️
:::
::: {.step-title}
Fit Model
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Train gradient boosting on the training set
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🔮
:::
::: {.step-title}
Predict
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Apply model to the test set it has never seen
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
✅
:::
::: {.step-title}
Evaluate
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Confusion matrix, accuracy, feature importance
:::
:::
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Why split the data?** If you evaluate a model on the same data it trained on, it is like grading a student on questions they already saw. The test set is the [final exam with new questions]{.accent}.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is the five-step workflow. Step one: prepare the data --- explore it, handle missing values, understand what each column means. Step two: split the data into a training set and a test set. The model learns from the training set and gets evaluated on the test set --- data it has never seen. Step three: fit the model --- this is where gradient boosting builds its hundreds of trees. Step four: predict --- apply the trained model to the test set. Step five: evaluate --- look at the confusion matrix, accuracy metrics, and feature importance.

The most important concept here is the train/test split. Why do we split the data? Because if you evaluate a model on the same data it trained on, you are measuring memorization, not generalization. It is like giving a student the final exam, then grading them on the exact same questions. Of course they will score well --- they have seen the answers. The test set is a final exam with new questions. If the model performs well on data it has never seen, you can trust that it has learned real patterns, not just memorized the training data.

Cross-validation takes this further. Instead of one split, it does five or ten different splits and averages the results. This gives you a more reliable estimate of how the model will perform on truly new data. Claude will handle cross-validation automatically when you ask for it.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the simulated demo.]
:::

---

## Demo: Prompting Claude to Build a Model {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Step 1: Explore the Data
:::
PROMPT: I have a CSV file with customer data. Each row is a customer.
The columns include tenure, monthly_charges, total_charges,
contract_type, payment_method, internet_service, and a column
called "churn" that is Yes or No.

Please explore this data: how many rows, any missing values,
and what does the distribution of churn look like?
:::

::: {.notes}
[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: This is a live demo. Open Claude and upload the customer churn CSV dataset. If the live demo fails, use these slides as the walkthrough and narrate what Claude would produce.

Start with this exact prompt. Claude will return a summary: number of rows, columns, missing values, and the class balance of the churn variable. Point out the class imbalance if it exists --- typically 70-75% stay vs. 25-30% churn. Explain why this matters: if 75% of customers stay, a model that always predicts "stay" would be 75% accurate but completely useless.]

Let me walk you through what this looks like in practice. I am going to show you a conversation with Claude where we build a predictive model step by step. In the hands-on exercise, you will do this yourself.

Step one: give Claude context about your data and ask it to explore. Notice I am not writing any code. I am describing the data in plain English and asking Claude to do the exploration. This is the same maker/checker pattern from Session 2 --- AI does the work, you evaluate the output.

[TIMING: 2 minutes on this slide. Move to Claude's response.]
:::

---

## Demo: Claude's Exploration Output {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Claude's Response (simulated)
:::
Here's what I found in your dataset:

**Dataset overview:**
- 7,043 customers, 21 columns
- No missing values (after converting 11 blank TotalCharges to 0)

**Churn distribution:**
- Did not churn: 5,174 (73.5%)
- Churned: 1,869 (26.5%)

**Key observations:**
- Month-to-month contracts churn at 42% vs. 11% for 2-year contracts
- Customers with fiber optic internet churn at 42% vs. 25% overall
- Average tenure for churned customers: 18 months vs. 38 months
  for retained customers

⚠️ The dataset is imbalanced (73/27 split). I'll account for
this when building the model.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is what Claude produces. It found 7,043 customers with 21 columns. No missing values after cleaning. And here is the critical finding: the data is imbalanced. Seventy-three percent of customers did not churn, twenty-seven percent did.

Why does imbalance matter? If I built a model that always predicts "no churn," it would be seventy-three percent accurate. That sounds decent, but it is completely useless --- it never identifies anyone who is actually at risk. Claude flags this and says it will account for it in the model. This is the kind of thing you need to watch for when evaluating model output.

Also notice the early insights: month-to-month contracts churn at forty-two percent, and tenure for churned customers is half that of retained customers. These are not predictions yet --- they are patterns in the data that tell you the model has something to work with.

[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to the model-building prompt.]
:::

---

## Demo: Building the Model {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Step 2: Build and Evaluate
:::
PROMPT: Build a gradient boosting classifier to predict churn.
Use an 80/20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation.

Show me:
1. The confusion matrix on the test set
2. Precision, recall, and F1 score
3. The top 10 most important features
4. Cross-validation scores

Explain the results in business terms, not just statistics.
:::

::: {.notes}
Step two: ask Claude to build the model. Notice the specificity of this prompt. I am not just saying "build a model." I am specifying the algorithm (gradient boosting), the split (80/20), the validation approach (5-fold cross-validation), and exactly what outputs I want. I am also asking Claude to explain results in business terms --- not just statistics.

This is a skill you are developing: writing precise prompts that produce evaluable output. The more specific your request, the more useful the response. If you just say "predict churn," Claude might use any algorithm and show you outputs you cannot interpret. By specifying what you want, you set yourself up to evaluate the results.

[TIMING: 1 minute. Move to Claude's model output.]
:::

---

## Demo: Model Results {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Claude's Model Output (simulated)
:::
**Model:** GradientBoostingClassifier (200 trees, max depth 4)

**Confusion Matrix (test set, 1,409 customers):**
                Predicted Stay    Predicted Churn
Actual Stay         896               139
Actual Churn        102               272

**Metrics:**
- Accuracy: 82.9%
- Precision (churn): 66.2%  — when we predict churn, we're right 66% of the time
- Recall (churn): 72.7%     — we catch 73% of actual churners
- F1 Score: 69.3%

**Cross-validation:** 81.4% ± 1.8% (stable across folds)
:::

::: {.notes}
Here are the results. The model achieves 82.9 percent accuracy on the test set. But accuracy alone is not enough --- remember, always predicting "stay" would give us 73.5 percent accuracy. We need to look deeper.

The confusion matrix is the key output. Let me walk through it. Top-left: 896 customers who actually stayed and the model correctly predicted "stay." Top-right: 139 customers who actually stayed but the model predicted "churn" --- these are false positives. Bottom-left: 102 customers who actually churned but the model predicted "stay" --- these are false negatives, and they are the most dangerous. Bottom-right: 272 customers who actually churned and the model correctly caught them --- true positives.

I will unpack the confusion matrix in detail on the next slide. For now, note the cross-validation score: 81.4 percent plus or minus 1.8 percent. This is stable across folds, which means the model is generalizing well, not just memorizing one particular train/test split.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the confusion matrix explanation.]
:::

---

## Evaluating Models {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Now that you have seen what a model produces, let us talk about how to evaluate it. This is where the maker/checker framework applies most directly. The AI built the model --- now you need to decide whether to trust it.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Reading a Confusion Matrix {.shrink}

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
What the Matrix Tells You
:::

- **True Positives (272):** Correctly predicted churn --- these are your wins
- **True Negatives (896):** Correctly predicted stay --- no action needed
- **False Positives (139):** Predicted churn, but customer stayed --- unnecessary retention spend
- **False Negatives (102):** Predicted stay, but customer churned --- [missed opportunity, the costly error]{.accent}
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
The Business Question
:::

- What is the **cost** of each error type?
- False positive: you offer a discount to someone who was staying anyway (~$50/customer?)
- False negative: you lose a customer worth $1,200/year in revenue
- [False negatives are 24x more costly]{.amber} --- optimize for recall
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The confusion matrix is not just a statistics table --- it is a business decision tool. Let me show you why.

We have two types of errors. False positives: 139 customers the model flagged as likely to churn, but they were actually going to stay. What is the cost? You might offer them a retention discount unnecessarily --- say fifty dollars each. That is about seven thousand dollars.

False negatives: 102 customers the model predicted would stay, but they actually churned. What is the cost? Each lost customer represents maybe twelve hundred dollars per year in revenue. That is a hundred and twenty-two thousand dollars in lost revenue.

False negatives are twenty-four times more costly than false positives in this example. That changes how you evaluate the model. You do not want the model with the highest accuracy --- you want the model that catches the most churners, even if it means more false alarms. This is the precision-recall tradeoff, and it is a business decision, not a statistical one.

When you evaluate a model, the first question is not "what is the accuracy?" It is "what is the cost of each type of error?" That determines which metric matters most.

[TIMING: 4 minutes. Move to precision vs recall.]
:::

---

## Precision vs. Recall: A Business Tradeoff

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Precision (66.2%)
:::

- "When we predict churn, how often are we right?"
- 272 correct out of 411 churn predictions
- Higher precision = fewer wasted retention offers
- [Optimize for precision when false positives are expensive]{.accent}
- Example: fraud alerts that freeze customer accounts
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Recall (72.7%)
:::

- "Of all actual churners, how many did we catch?"
- 272 caught out of 374 actual churners
- Higher recall = fewer missed churners
- [Optimize for recall when false negatives are expensive]{.amber}
- Example: cancer screening, churn prevention, fraud in high-value accounts
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
You cannot maximize both. Catching more churners (higher recall) means more false alarms (lower precision). The right balance depends on your business costs.
:::

::: {.notes}
Precision and recall are inversely related --- you cannot maximize both simultaneously. Increasing recall means casting a wider net, which catches more true churners but also flags more customers who were not going to churn. Increasing precision means being more selective, which reduces false alarms but misses more actual churners.

The right tradeoff depends entirely on your business context. For churn prevention, where a lost customer is worth far more than a wasted discount, you optimize for recall. For fraud detection where blocking a legitimate transaction angers a customer, you might balance more toward precision. For cancer screening, you overwhelmingly optimize for recall --- you never want to miss a true positive.

This is a judgment call that AI cannot make for you. The model can give you any tradeoff you want by adjusting a threshold. Your job is deciding which tradeoff is right for the business.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to regression metrics.]
:::

---

## Regression Metrics: R² and RMSE

::: {.notes}
This section covers regression metrics for completeness. If you are running short on time, this can be skipped --- today's hands-on exercise uses classification only. Point students to these slides as reference material.
:::

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
R² (R-squared)
:::

- "How much of the variation does the model explain?"
- R² = 0.85 means the model explains 85% of variation in the target
- R² = 1.0 is a perfect model (suspicious --- probably data leakage)
- R² = 0.0 means the model is no better than predicting the average
- [Business rule of thumb: R² > 0.7 is usually useful for planning]{.accent}
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
:::

- "On average, how far off are the predictions?"
- RMSE = $4,200 means predictions are off by ~$4,200 on average
- In the same units as the target variable (dollars, units, etc.)
- [Ask: "Is this error margin acceptable for the business decision?"]{.amber}
- RMSE of $4,200 on $50K deals is fine; on $8K deals, it is not
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
For regression problems --- predicting continuous numbers --- you use different metrics. R-squared tells you how much of the variation in the target your model explains. An R-squared of 0.85 means the model captures eighty-five percent of the variation in, say, quarterly revenue. The remaining fifteen percent is noise the model cannot explain.

RMSE --- root mean squared error --- tells you how far off the predictions are on average, in the same units as your target. If you are predicting deal values and the RMSE is four thousand two hundred dollars, that means the model's predictions are off by about four thousand two hundred dollars on average.

The business question for RMSE is always: is this error margin acceptable for the decision I am making? An RMSE of four thousand two hundred dollars on fifty-thousand-dollar deals is fine for forecasting. The same RMSE on eight-thousand-dollar deals means the model is off by more than fifty percent on average --- probably not useful.

You will not build a regression model in today's exercise, but you will encounter these metrics when you or your team work on forecasting and demand planning.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to feature importance.]
:::

---

## Feature Importance: What Drives Predictions? {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Top 10 Features (from our churn model)
:::
Feature                    Importance
─────────────────────────────────────
1. contract_type              0.231    ← Month-to-month is the #1 risk
2. tenure                     0.189    ← New customers churn more
3. monthly_charges            0.142    ← Higher bills → higher churn
4. internet_service_fiber     0.098    ← Fiber customers churn more
5. total_charges              0.074
6. payment_method_electronic  0.062
7. online_security_no         0.048
8. tech_support_no            0.041
9. online_backup_no           0.035
10. paperless_billing         0.028
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**The maker/checker question:** Do these make business sense? Contract type and tenure as top predictors? [Yes --- that passes the smell test.]{.accent} If "customer_id" were the top feature, something is very wrong.
:::

::: {.notes}
Feature importance tells you which variables the model relies on most. This is one of the most valuable outputs for business decision-making, separate from the predictions themselves.

Look at this list. Contract type is the number one predictor of churn. Month-to-month customers churn at much higher rates than those on annual or two-year contracts. Tenure is second --- newer customers are more likely to leave. Monthly charges is third --- customers paying higher bills are more sensitive to competitors.

Now apply the maker/checker framework. Do these features make business sense? Absolutely. If you asked any experienced sales or customer success leader "what drives churn?", they would say contract type, tenure, and price sensitivity. The model confirms domain expertise.

But here is the red flag to watch for. If "customer_id" appeared as a top feature, that would mean the model is memorizing individual customers, not learning patterns. If "zip_code" appeared as number one, it might be exploiting a data artifact rather than a real business driver. Feature importance is your most powerful tool for catching models that are technically accurate but fundamentally flawed.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the maker/checker framework for models.]
:::

---

## The Maker/Checker Framework for Models

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
1. Better Than Baseline?
:::

- Does the model beat a naive approach?
- Baseline for churn: predict "stay" for everyone → 73.5% accuracy
- Our model: 82.9% accuracy
- [9.4 percentage points better]{.accent} --- real improvement
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
2. Features Make Sense?
:::

- Do the top predictors match domain knowledge?
- Contract type, tenure, charges → [makes sense]{.amber}
- Customer ID, row number → [data leakage]{.amber}
- Ask a domain expert: "Do these surprise you?"
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
3. Generalizes Well?
:::

- Cross-validation: 81.4% ± 1.8%
- Training accuracy: 85.2%
- Small gap → model is [generalizing]{.accent}
- If train = 99% but CV = 60% → overfitting
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is your three-question checklist for evaluating any predictive model. You do not need to understand the math. You need to ask these three questions.

Question one: does the model beat a baseline? The simplest baseline for churn is "predict everyone stays" --- that gives you 73.5 percent accuracy for free. Our model gets 82.9 percent. That is a real improvement of 9.4 percentage points. If a model only beats the baseline by one or two points, it may not be worth the complexity.

Question two: do the important features make business sense? We already covered this. Contract type, tenure, and monthly charges as top predictors? That matches what any experienced manager would tell you. If the top features do not make sense, the model may be exploiting a data artifact.

Question three: does the model generalize? Compare the cross-validation score to the training score. Our model gets 85.2 percent on training data and 81.4 percent on cross-validation. That is a small gap, which means the model learned real patterns, not memorized the training data. If you saw training accuracy of 99 percent but cross-validation accuracy of 60 percent, that model is memorizing, not learning. It will fail on new data.

Three questions. That is your entire evaluation framework. AI builds the model --- you ask these three questions.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to red flags.]
:::

---

## Red Flags: When Not to Trust the Model {.shrink}

::: {.four-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
🚩 Too Good to Be True
:::

- 99% accuracy usually means data leakage
- The target variable leaked into the features
- Example: including "cancellation_date" to predict churn
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
🚩 Nonsense Features
:::

- Customer ID, row number, or timestamp as top features
- The model is memorizing, not learning
- Always check feature importance before trusting predictions
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
🚩 Big Train/Test Gap
:::

- Train accuracy: 98%
- Test accuracy: 65%
- The model memorized training data and fails on new data
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
🚩 Unstable Cross-Val
:::

- Scores: 85%, 62%, 78%, 91%, 55%
- High variance across folds
- Model is sensitive to which data it sees --- not reliable
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Four red flags that should make you stop and investigate before deploying a model.

First: too good to be true. If you see 99 percent accuracy, do not celebrate --- investigate. Almost always, this means data leakage: information about the outcome leaked into the input features. The classic example is including "cancellation_date" as a feature when predicting churn. Of course the model is perfect --- it can see the answer in the input.

Second: nonsense features at the top of the importance list. Customer ID, row number, account creation timestamp --- these should never be meaningful predictors. If they are, the model is memorizing specific customers rather than learning generalizable patterns.

Third: a large gap between training and test performance. If the model gets 98 percent on training data but 65 percent on test data, it has memorized the training set. It learned the noise, not the signal.

Fourth: unstable cross-validation scores. If the model gets 85 percent on one fold and 55 percent on another, it is highly sensitive to which data it sees. That is not a model you can rely on for business decisions.

Any of these red flags means you need to go back and investigate before trusting the predictions.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the hands-on exercise.]
:::

---

## Hands-On: Build a Predictive Model {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Now it is your turn. You are going to use Claude to build a predictive model from start to finish. I will walk you through five guided steps, and you will work through them at your own pace. The goal is not just to get a result --- it is to practice the evaluation skills we just covered.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Exercise Setup {.shrink}

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
What You Need
:::

- Open Claude at [claude.ai](https://claude.ai)
- Download the customer churn dataset (link in chat)
- Upload the CSV to Claude
- Follow the five guided steps on the next slide
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Time:** 20 minutes. Work individually. I will circulate to help.

**Goal:** Not just to build a model, but to [evaluate it]{.accent} using the framework we just learned. Can you answer the three maker/checker questions about your model?
:::

::: {.notes}
[HANDS-ON SETUP: Post the dataset link in the Zoom chat. Give students 1 minute to download and open Claude. The dataset is a standard telecom customer churn dataset with about 7,000 rows and 21 columns. If students have trouble uploading, remind them to use the attachment/paperclip icon in Claude.]

Here is how we will spend the next twenty minutes. You have a customer churn dataset. You are going to upload it to Claude and work through five guided steps. The goal is not just to build a model --- any prompt will produce a model. The goal is to evaluate it using the three-question framework: better than baseline, features make sense, and generalizes well.

Work individually. I will circulate and help. If you get stuck on any step, raise your hand or post in chat.

[TIMING: 2 minutes for setup. Then advance to the guided steps.]
:::

---

## Five Guided Steps {.shrink}

::: {.step-flow-5}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1️⃣
:::
::: {.step-title}
Explore
:::
::: {.step-desc}
"Explore this data. How many rows? Missing values? What's the churn rate?"
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2️⃣
:::
::: {.step-title}
Build
:::
::: {.step-desc}
"Build a gradient boosting classifier with 80/20 split and cross-validation."
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3️⃣
:::
::: {.step-title}
Evaluate
:::
::: {.step-desc}
"Show me the confusion matrix. What's the false negative rate?"
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4️⃣
:::
::: {.step-title}
Interpret
:::
::: {.step-desc}
"What are the top 10 features? Do they make business sense?"
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
5️⃣
:::
::: {.step-title}
Challenge
:::
::: {.step-desc}
"What would a skeptical VP push back on? How would you improve this?"
:::
:::
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Bonus prompt:** "If I could only act on the top 200 highest-risk customers, which ones would you flag and why?"
:::

::: {.notes}
Here are your five steps. Step one: explore the data. Ask Claude to tell you what is in the dataset, how many rows, any missing values, and what the churn rate looks like. Step two: build the model. Ask for a gradient boosting classifier with an 80/20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation. Step three: evaluate. Ask for the confusion matrix and specifically the false negative rate --- how many churners is the model missing? Step four: interpret the feature importance. Do the top features make sense? Step five: challenge the model. Ask Claude what a skeptical VP would push back on. This is the maker/checker step.

The bonus prompt is my favorite: "If I could only act on the top 200 highest-risk customers, which ones would you flag and why?" This forces the model output into a concrete business action. It is not about accuracy in the abstract --- it is about which specific customers get a retention call.

You have twenty minutes. Go.

[TIMING: 18-20 minutes for the exercise. Circulate the room. Common issues: (1) Students who upload the CSV but do not describe it --- remind them to give Claude context. (2) Students who get a model but do not evaluate it --- push them to steps 3-5. (3) Students who get stuck on errors --- usually a Claude timeout on large code blocks, just ask Claude to try again.]
:::

---

## Example: Step 5 in Action {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Prompt: Challenge the Model
:::
PROMPT: What would a skeptical VP of Customer Success push back on
if I presented this model? What are the weaknesses?
:::

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
Claude's Response (simulated)
:::
A skeptical VP would likely raise these concerns:

1. **"We're missing 27% of churners."** The recall of 72.7% means
   102 customers who churned were not flagged. If each lost customer
   costs $1,200/year, that's $122K in missed revenue.

2. **"Contract type is the top feature — we already know that."**
   The model confirms what the team already suspects. The VP wants
   to know: what does the model tell us that we DON'T already know?
   → Look at features 4-10 for non-obvious insights.

3. **"This is trained on historical data."** If we just changed our
   pricing or launched a new product, past churn patterns may not
   predict future behavior. The model needs retraining regularly.

4. **"What do we DO with the predictions?"** A model is only useful
   if it drives action. What's the intervention for flagged customers?
   Who owns the follow-up?
:::

::: {.notes}
This is what step five looks like in practice. When you ask Claude to play the role of a skeptical VP, it surfaces exactly the right concerns. The recall gap means we are missing a quarter of churners. The top features confirm existing intuition rather than revealing new insights. The model is trained on historical data that may not reflect current conditions. And most importantly: what do you actually do with the predictions?

That fourth point is the most important. A model that predicts churn is only valuable if someone acts on the predictions. Who gets the list of at-risk customers? What is the intervention? Is it an email, a phone call, a discount offer? How do you measure whether the intervention worked? These are business questions, not technical ones. The model is the easy part. The organizational response is the hard part.

[TIMING: This slide is shown during the exercise wrap-up, around minute 55. Spend 2-3 minutes on it before moving to the debrief.]
:::

---

## Exercise Debrief

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Quick Show of Hands
:::

- Who got a model accuracy above 80%?
- Who found that contract type was the top feature?
- Who tried the bonus prompt about flagging specific customers?
- Who found something in the feature importance that surprised them?
:::

::: {.explainer}
The model is the easy part. The hard part is: [what do you do with the predictions?]{.accent}
:::

::: {.notes}
[DEBRIEF (3-5 minutes): Quick show of hands for each question. Most students will have gotten accuracy above 80% --- the dataset is well-behaved. Most will have found contract type as the top feature. Push the conversation toward the fourth question: what surprised you in the feature importance?

If time permits, ask 1-2 students: "What did Claude say when you asked what a skeptical VP would push back on?" Listen for whether students are starting to apply the maker/checker mindset to model output.

Bridge to the breakout: "You just built a model for churn prediction. Now I want you to think about your own company. Where could prediction help? What would you predict, and what would you do with the predictions?"]
:::

---

## From Model to App {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
You built a model. Now let us make it usable. A prediction sitting in a Jupyter notebook helps no one. The next step is putting it into the hands of the people who make decisions --- and that means turning it into an application.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Deploy Your Model as a Dashboard {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
The prompt (typed into Claude Code on the AI+Code Lab)
:::
```
Take the churn prediction model we just built. Create an
interactive web app where a user can enter customer attributes
(contract length, monthly charges, tenure, support tickets)
and get a churn probability with the top 3 risk factors.
```
:::

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
What the App Produces
:::
- Input form with sliders and dropdowns for customer attributes
- A prediction output: "Churn probability: 73% --- High Risk"
- Feature importance bar chart showing the top 3 risk factors for this customer
- Recommended actions: "Offer contract upgrade," "Assign dedicated support rep," "Schedule executive check-in"
- A sales manager could use this every day to prioritize retention outreach
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "This is the full arc. We went from raw data to a predictive model to a deployable application --- all with AI assistance. The model runs inside the app. A sales manager could use this every day to prioritize retention outreach."

"Notice the prompt: one sentence. Paste the model code, ask for a web app. Claude Code handles the rest --- it picks the framework, builds the input form, the prediction logic, the visualization, the recommended actions. It might use Streamlit, Gradio, or FastAPI depending on the task. The result is a working web app that anyone on the team can use without touching code or understanding the model."

"This is what 'predictions need action' looks like in practice. The model is embedded in a tool that surfaces recommendations to the person who can act on them."

This raises the same deployment questions from Session 5: where does this model run? Cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid? The DUNNIXER framework applies to ML deployment just as it does to data agents. For your capstone, if you include predictive modeling, specify the deployment approach.
:::

---

## The Full Arc {.step-flow}

::: {.step-flow}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1
:::
::: {.step-title}
Data
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Raw customer records from enterprise systems
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2
:::
::: {.step-title}
Model
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Gradient boosting trained on historical patterns
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3
:::
::: {.step-title}
Dashboard
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Web app with inputs, predictions, and actions
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4
:::
::: {.step-title}
Decision
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Sales manager calls the right customer today
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
From raw data to a decision tool. This is what AI + code gives you: not just answers, but [applications]{.accent}.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Data, model, dashboard, decision. That is the full arc. You started this session with a CSV file. You built a predictive model. You could deploy it as a web app. And the result is a decision tool --- a sales manager opens the app, enters a customer's attributes, and gets a recommendation."

"This is optional homework on the AI+Code Lab for those who want to try it. The prompt is straightforward --- paste the model code, ask for a web app. Claude Code picks the framework and builds it. But the concept is what matters: you can go from data to deployable application in a single sitting."

"When you think about your capstone, this is the level of specificity that scores well. Not 'we will use ML' but 'we will build a churn prediction dashboard that the customer success team uses every morning to prioritize outreach.'"

[TIMING: 3 minutes for these three slides. Then move to the breakout.]
:::

---

## Breakout: Prediction Opportunities {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Time for the breakout. You have practiced building and evaluating a model on someone else's data. Now I want you to think about your own organization. Where could prediction create value? What data would you need? And critically --- what would you do with the predictions?

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Breakout Instructions {.shrink}

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Breakout Exercise (10 minutes, groups of 3-4)
:::

Each person identifies 1-2 prediction problems at their company:

1. **What would you predict?** (Be specific: "customer churn" not "make better predictions")
2. **Classification or regression?**
3. **What data would you need?** Do you have it? Where does it live?
4. **What is the cost of a false positive vs. false negative?**
5. **What would you DO with the predictions?** Who acts on them?
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Report back:** Each group shares the most valuable prediction opportunity and the biggest data obstacle.
:::

::: {.notes}
[BREAKOUT INSTRUCTIONS: Create breakout rooms of 3-4 people. Display this slide in the rooms. Each person should identify one or two prediction problems at their company and answer the five questions. The group then picks the most valuable opportunity and the biggest obstacle to share with the full class.

TIMING: 10 minutes in breakout rooms. Then 5 minutes for report-back --- call on 3-4 groups. Push for specificity: "You said 'predict demand' --- demand for what? What data would you use? What would you do differently if the model said demand would spike next month?"

Common patterns you will hear: churn prediction (most common), lead scoring, demand forecasting, employee attrition, equipment failure prediction. The most interesting discussions will be about question 4 (false positive vs. false negative costs) and question 5 (what action follows the prediction).

Listen for the data obstacle theme: most students will discover that the data they need either does not exist in a clean form, lives in a system they cannot access, or would need to be combined from multiple sources. This connects back to Sessions 3-4 --- the data access problem is real.]
:::

---

## Report Back

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Most Valuable Predictions
:::

- What patterns emerged across groups?
- Which prediction opportunities had the clearest ROI?
- Did anyone identify a prediction that changes a decision process?
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Biggest Data Obstacles
:::

- Data does not exist in clean form
- Lives in systems you cannot access
- Would need to combine multiple sources
- [Sound familiar from Sessions 3-4?]{.amber}
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
The Action Question
:::

- A prediction without action is a report
- Who receives the predictions?
- What decision changes?
- [This is the capstone question]{.accent}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
[REPORT-BACK: Call on 3-4 groups. Capture the themes on screen or verbally. The three columns on this slide will likely match what you hear: valuable opportunities, data obstacles, and the action question.

Key teaching moment: emphasize the rightmost card. A prediction without action is just another report. The value of predictive modeling is not the model itself --- it is the decision that changes because of the model. "We predict this customer will churn" is only valuable if someone calls that customer, offers a retention deal, or escalates to account management. "We predict demand will spike" is only valuable if someone adjusts inventory, staffing, or marketing spend.

This connects directly to the capstone. Students should be thinking: "In my capstone strategy, if I include predictive modeling, what specific decision does it improve and who acts on it?"

TIMING: 5 minutes for report-back. Then bridge to capstone.]
:::

---

## Bridge to Capstone {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
We are in the home stretch. Let me connect everything we have done to where you are headed next session.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Your Full Toolkit

::: {.step-flow-6}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
💬
:::
::: {.step-title}
Sessions 1-2
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Personal productivity: charts, docs, analysis
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🗄️
:::
::: {.step-title}
Session 3
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Enterprise data: structured queries + document Q&A
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
📊
:::
::: {.step-title}
Session 4
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Reporting pipelines: data → analysis → deliverable
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🏗️
:::
::: {.step-title}
Session 5
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Deployment: architecture, security, build vs. buy
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🔍
:::
::: {.step-title}
Session 6
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Trust: failure modes, red-teaming, governance
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
🔮
:::
::: {.step-title}
Session 7
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Prediction: classification, regression, evaluation
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
Your capstone strategy can draw on [any combination]{.accent} of these capabilities. Next session: you present.
:::

::: {.notes}
Let me put today in context. Over seven sessions, you have built a comprehensive AI toolkit. Sessions 1 and 2 gave you personal productivity --- using AI to generate charts, documents, and analyses. Session 3 connected AI to enterprise data, both structured databases and document Q&A. Session 4 showed you how to build reporting pipelines. Session 5 covered deployment architecture and security. Session 6 gave you the tools to evaluate reliability and design governance. And today, Session 7, added predictive modeling.

Your capstone strategy can draw on any combination of these capabilities. Maybe your use case is primarily personal productivity. Maybe it involves enterprise data access. Maybe it includes predictive modeling. Maybe it combines all three. The choice depends on your organization's needs, data readiness, and governance maturity.

Next Monday is Session 8 --- you present. Let me tell you what that looks like and what you need to finalize this week.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to homework.]
:::

---

## Capstone Preparation

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Your Strategy Document (1-2 pages)
:::

- Problem statement: what business problem does AI solve?
- Proposed solution: which AI capabilities do you use?
- Deployment approach: cloud, on-premise, or hybrid?
- Governance plan: access controls, verification, compliance
- 90-day pilot plan: concrete first steps
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Your Presentation (5 minutes)
:::

- Pitch to your executive team (your breakout group)
- Breakout groups vote on strongest strategy
- 5 winners present to full class
- Instructor feedback on: scoped tightly enough? Business case credible? Governance adequate?
:::
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Self-check:** Use the evaluation rubric provided with the template. Does your Day 1 action actually happen on Day 1?
:::

::: {.notes}
Here is what you need to finalize before next session. Your strategy document should be one to two pages using the capstone template. It covers five things: the problem you are solving, the AI capabilities you are using, the deployment approach, your governance plan, and a concrete 90-day pilot plan.

Your presentation is five minutes. You are pitching to your executive team --- your breakout group plays that role. Each breakout group votes on the strongest strategy, and the five winners present to the full class. I will give feedback on whether the pilot is scoped tightly enough, whether the business case is credible, and whether the governance plan is adequate.

The most important self-check: does your Day 1 action actually happen on Day 1? If your "first step" requires IT approval, budget allocation, and a vendor evaluation, it is not a Day 1 action. A real Day 1 action is something you can do Monday morning without asking anyone's permission.

[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to homework summary.]
:::

---

## Homework {.shrink}

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Before Session 8 (Monday)
:::

1. **Finalize your strategy document** (1-2 pages using the capstone template)
2. **Prepare your 5-minute presentation** --- use the evaluation rubric to self-check
3. **Consider predictive modeling** --- if it fits your use case, add it to your strategy with specifics: what would you predict, what data would you need, what action would follow?
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
**Optional:** Demos 6-7 show building a RAG system and combining it with a database agent. Relevant if your capstone includes document AI.

**Optional:** Deploy your churn model as an interactive dashboard on the AI+Code Lab. The prompt is one sentence.
:::

::: {.notes}
Three homework items. First, finalize your strategy document. Use the template and the rubric. Second, prepare your presentation --- five minutes, clear and concise. Third, consider whether predictive modeling belongs in your strategy. If it does, be specific: what would you predict, what data do you need, and what action follows the predictions? Vague statements like "we could use ML" will not score well on the rubric.

The optional viewing is Demos 6 and 7, which show building a RAG system and combining it with a database agent. These are relevant if your capstone involves document AI or if you want to see how multiple AI capabilities integrate.

[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to the closing slide.]
:::

---

## Key Takeaways {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Let me leave you with three takeaways from today.

[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider --- advance immediately.]
:::

---

## Three Things to Remember

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
AI Builds the Model
:::

- You do not need to write code
- You do not need to understand the math
- You need to write clear prompts and [evaluate the output]{.amber}
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Three Questions to Ask
:::

- Better than baseline?
- Features make business sense?
- Generalizes to new data?
- [If yes to all three, proceed with caution]{.accent}
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Predictions Need Action
:::

- A model without action is just a report
- Who receives predictions?
- What decision changes?
- [This is what separates strategy from spectacle]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Three takeaways. First: AI builds the model, you evaluate it. You did this today --- you prompted Claude, it wrote the code, and you evaluated the confusion matrix, feature importance, and cross-validation scores. That is the workflow.

Second: three questions. Does it beat the baseline? Do the features make sense? Does it generalize? If the answer to all three is yes, you have a model worth exploring further. If any answer is no, investigate before trusting the predictions.

Third: predictions need action. This is the most important takeaway. A churn model is only valuable if someone calls the at-risk customers. A demand forecast is only valuable if someone adjusts inventory. Your capstone should specify not just what AI does, but what humans do differently because of it.

See you Monday for your presentations. Come prepared --- this is your chance to synthesize everything from the past seven sessions into a strategy you can actually take back to your organization.

[TIMING: 2 minutes. End of session.]
:::
